then.message: The contract contains a termination clause.
then.recommendation: Confirm the termination clause covers both for-cause and for-convenience scenarios, if intended, and that post-termination obligations (data return, final payment) are addressed.
then.rationale: A termination clause establishes when and how either party may end the agreement, which is foundational to managing exit risk.

then.message: The contract lacks a termination clause.
then.recommendation: Add a termination clause specifying at minimum a for-cause right (with a cure period) and the effects of termination.
then.rationale: Without an express termination clause, ending the contract early depends on proving a material breach at common law, which is slower and less certain than a negotiated right.

then.message: Termination notice period for convenience is less than 30 days, creating operational risk.
then.recommendation: Extend the termination-for-convenience notice period to at least 30 days (longer for complex or service-dependent relationships) to allow for orderly transition.
then.rationale: A short notice period gives the non-terminating party little time to arrange a replacement or wind down dependent operations.

then.message: Termination for convenience is unilateral, which creates unfair leverage.
then.recommendation: Make the termination-for-convenience right mutual, or add compensating protections (e.g., a longer notice period or termination fee) if one-sided termination is commercially necessary.
then.rationale: A one-sided termination-for-convenience right gives one party unilateral leverage to exit at will while binding the other party for the full term.

then.message: Cure period for breach is less than 15 days, creating risk.
then.recommendation: Extend the cure period for breach to at least 15-30 days, scaled to the complexity of the obligation likely to be breached.
then.rationale: A short cure period increases the risk that an otherwise-remediable, non-material lapse escalates into a contract-ending event.
